text: The surgeon opens the bursa omentalis beneath the gastroepiploic artery, fixing the dorsal part of the stomach to the abdominal wall with sutures. Traction is applied through the mesocolon using a band, which is led outside the trocar, preparing it for future robotic instrument use. This provides a clear view of the bursa omentalis and exposes the pancreatic body and tail by reopening the retroperitoneum.

The surgeon applies gentle traction and counter traction to dissect the border beneath the pancreas, revealing the IPMN. The pancreatic vein is sought, typically located in the dorsal cranial part of the pancreas, with small branches visible that may cause bleeding.

The surgeon locates the splenic artery at the upper border, placing a vessel loop for traction and potential clamping in case of bleeding. The dissection proceeds to the splenic vein, utilizing the robotic platform to adjust the camera angle for better visualization of the small venous branches. Larger branches are transected using a vessel sealer.
A band is placed underneath the pancreatic tail, ensuring the splenic vein is not looped. A clip is applied for traction, and the dissection proceeds underneath the pancreas towards the spleen.

The surgeon addresses the splenic artery, using a vessel sealer or small clips to manage its few branches. After freeing the pancreatic tail from the artery and vein, small adhesions are removed with the vessel sealer. A stapling device with a black cartridge is then used to slowly compress and staple the pancreatic tail, allowing the parenchyma to adjust to the tension.

Bleeding occurs in the surgical field, which is controlled using a single mattress stitch with the aid of the robotic platform. The specimen is placed in a bag and removed through a small incision. The surgeon inspects the operation field for hemostasis, confirming that the artery and vein remain intact, and places a drain for postoperative care.
